verses.text: The famine grows worse. The grain from Egypt is gone. Jacob tells his sons: go back, buy us a little more food. He has been refusing this trip for as long as he could.
The famine grows worse. The grain from Egypt is gone. Jacob tells his sons: go back, buy us a little more food. He has been refusing this trip for as long as he could.

Judah answers: the man warned us clearly — you will not see my face unless your brother is with you. If you will send him with us, we will go. If not, we will not — there is no use. The bargain is settled by the man in Egypt, not by us.

Judah answers: the man warned us clearly — you will not see my face unless your brother is with you. If you will send him with us, we will go. If not, we will not — there is no use. The bargain is settled by the man in Egypt, not by us.

Judah answers: the man warned us clearly — you will not see my face unless your brother is with you. If you will send him with us, we will go. If not, we will not — there is no use. The bargain is settled by the man in Egypt, not by us.

Why have you dealt so ill with me, Israel says — telling the man you had another brother? They answer: he asked about our family carefully; how could we know he would demand we bring him down? Old patriarchal anger gives way to old patriarchal resignation.

Why have you dealt so ill with me, Israel says — telling the man you had another brother? They answer: he asked about our family carefully; how could we know he would demand we bring him down? Old patriarchal anger gives way to old patriarchal resignation.

Judah steps forward: send the boy with me. We will rise and go, that we may live and not die — we, you, and our little ones. I will be his guarantee. If I do not bring him back, let me bear the blame forever. If we had not delayed, we would already have been back.

Judah steps forward: send the boy with me. We will rise and go, that we may live and not die — we, you, and our little ones. I will be his guarantee. If I do not bring him back, let me bear the blame forever. If we had not delayed, we would already have been back.

Judah steps forward: send the boy with me. We will rise and go, that we may live and not die — we, you, and our little ones. I will be his guarantee. If I do not bring him back, let me bear the blame forever. If we had not delayed, we would already have been back.

Israel finally agrees. If it must be so, take a gift — balm, honey, spices, myrrh, nuts, almonds — the best of the land. Take double the money; perhaps the returned silver was a mistake. Take your brother. And may God Almighty give you mercy before the man. If I am bereaved of my children, I am bereaved. He releases what he cannot keep.

Israel finally agrees. If it must be so, take a gift — balm, honey, spices, myrrh, nuts, almonds — the best of the land. Take double the money; perhaps the returned silver was a mistake. Take your brother. And may God Almighty give you mercy before the man. If I am bereaved of my children, I am bereaved. He releases what he cannot keep.

Take your brother. And may God Almighty give you mercy before the man. If I am bereaved of my children, I am bereaved. He releases what he cannot keep.

Israel finally agrees. If it must be so, take a gift — balm, honey, spices, myrrh, nuts, almonds — the best of the land. Take double the money; perhaps the returned silver was a mistake. Take your brother. And may God Almighty give you mercy before the man. If I am bereaved of my children, I am bereaved. He releases what he cannot keep.

The brothers take the gift, the double money, and Benjamin, and go down to stand before Joseph. When Joseph sees Benjamin, he tells his steward: bring these men home and prepare a meal. They will dine with me at noon.

The brothers take the gift, the double money, and Benjamin, and go down to stand before Joseph. When Joseph sees Benjamin, he tells his steward: bring these men home and prepare a meal. They will dine with me at noon.

The steward brings them into Joseph's house. The brothers are afraid: it must be because of the money returned in our sacks last time. He will accuse us, take us as slaves, and seize our donkeys. Strange grace from earlier still looks like a trap.

The steward brings them into Joseph's house. The brothers are afraid: it must be because of the money returned in our sacks last time. He will accuse us, take us as slaves, and seize our donkeys. Strange grace from earlier still looks like a trap.

They speak with the steward at the door before going in: please, sir, we came down once to buy food. When we opened our sacks, our money was back in them — in full weight. We have brought it again, and more besides. We do not know who put it there.

They speak with the steward at the door before going in: please, sir, we came down once to buy food. When we opened our sacks, our money was back in them — in full weight. We have brought it again, and more besides. We do not know who put it there.

They speak with the steward at the door before going in: please, sir, we came down once to buy food. When we opened our sacks, our money was back in them — in full weight. We have brought it again, and more besides. We do not know who put it there.

They speak with the steward at the door before going in: please, sir, we came down once to buy food. When we opened our sacks, our money was back in them — in full weight. We have brought it again, and more besides. We do not know who put it there.

The steward says: peace to you, do not fear. Your God, and the God of your father, has given you treasure in your sacks. I had your money. Then he brings Simeon out to them. The bound brother is free.

He brings them into the house, gives them water to wash their feet, and feeds their donkeys. They prepare their gift, waiting for noon — they have heard they are to eat there. Hospitality, not arrest.

He brings them into the house, gives them water to wash their feet, and feeds their donkeys. They prepare their gift, waiting for noon — they have heard they are to eat there. Hospitality, not arrest.

Joseph comes home. They bring in the gift, bowing themselves to the ground — fulfilling the dream a second time. He asks after them tenderly: is your father well, the old man you spoke of? Is he still alive?

Joseph comes home. They bring in the gift, bowing themselves to the ground — fulfilling the dream a second time. He asks after them tenderly: is your father well, the old man you spoke of? Is he still alive?

Your servant our father is well, they say — he is still alive. They bow again. Then Joseph lifts his eyes and sees Benjamin, his own mother's son: is this your youngest brother? God be gracious to you, my son. The half-brother greets his full brother gently, in code.

Your servant our father is well, they say — he is still alive. They bow again. Then Joseph lifts his eyes and sees Benjamin, his own mother's son: is this your youngest brother? God be gracious to you, my son. The half-brother greets his full brother gently, in code.

Joseph hurries away — his heart yearns for his brother. He goes into his chamber and weeps. Then he washes his face, returns, controls himself, and says: serve the meal. The grown man behind the throne is still that seventeen-year-old in the pit.

Joseph hurries away — his heart yearns for his brother. He goes into his chamber and weeps. Then he washes his face, returns, controls himself, and says: serve the meal. The grown man behind the throne is still that seventeen-year-old in the pit.

Three tables: Joseph by himself, his brothers by themselves, the Egyptians by themselves — Egyptians would not eat with Hebrews; it was an abomination to them. The brothers are seated by birth order — firstborn to youngest — and they look at each other in wonder. Then Benjamin's portion comes out five times larger than the others'. They drink and grow merry with him, without yet knowing why.
